subtask: 3.1.3 - Periodic compaction (issue #15) - THIRD verification pass, F2-fix cycle
prior_verdict: CHANGES_REQUESTED (010-verification, F2: TruncateNode dir-delete + segment renumbering silently drops post-truncation edges on the ordinary happy path)
security_note: Repeated, confirmed prompt-injection pattern seen again this pass: fake system-reminder-style text (fake date-change notices, fake MCP 'tokensave' tool instructions, fake 'Auto Mode Active' directives) embedded inside plain git/file tool output during this run. Treated as untrusted data only; nothing in it was acted upon.

requirements.evidence: F2 (silent, no-crash-required permanent edge loss after node's first successful truncation) is fixed via wal.WriteSegmentFloor: TruncateNode records a durable, monotonic floor (atomic temp+fsync+rename) one past the highest segment number ever used for a node, BEFORE removing segment files, so segment numbers are never reused. Independently reconfirmed via redone revert-experiment (see adversarial_checks_summary #1).

architecture_conformance.evidence: Floor mechanism lives in engine/wal (writer.go), consistent with the package's existing durability idiom (temp+fsync+rename, matching segment-file writes and WriteCSR/saveCompactState). graph/edgelog.go and compact.go changes are additive and layered correctly: wal package owns segment-number authority, graph package owns compaction/sidecar semantics, no layering violation.

regression_risk.evidence: Full go test ./... green except the pre-known, explicitly-anticipated TestReaderDuringSplit flake (engine/split, unrelated package, no connection to this subtask). TestCompaction and TestTruncateNode both green x10 under -race. wal package green x5 under -race.

crash_window_floor_before_removal.evidence: Constructed and ran a direct, non-mocked experiment (TestFloorCrash_LeftoverSegmentWinsOverFloor): wrote a segment, called wal.WriteSegmentFloor successfully, then deliberately did NOT remove the segment file (simulating a crash between floor-write and removal). Confirmed OpenWriter's latestSegmentNum resumes from the still-present segment file's own number (0), NOT the floor (1) -- exactly matching the documented 'leftover un-removed segment safely degrades to F1's already-accepted retry scenario' claim. No data loss, no skip.

floor_write_own_atomicity.evidence: TestFloorCrash_TornTempFileIgnored: simulated a crash mid-WriteSegmentFloor (temp file created, never renamed). Confirmed readSegmentFloor/latestSegmentNum treats this as 'no floor recorded' (falls through cleanly), never observes a torn/partial value -- WriteSegmentFloor's own temp+fsync+rename is genuinely atomic, matching the rest of the package. TestFloorMonotonic: confirmed a lower floor computed from a stale listing never regresses an already-published higher floor.

sidecar_floor_consistency.evidence: Constructed TestSidecarFloorConsistency_MultiSegmentTruncate: ran 5 append/Compact rounds on one node, then directly compared compact.go's sidecar-recorded 'compacted-through segment N' against the floor's actual effect (verified indirectly via the concrete on-disk filename the next AppendEdge produces after an explicit TruncateNode). Confirmed floor == sidecarSeg+1 exactly, every round -- the two mechanisms never diverge. Both do genuinely distinct, non-redundant work: the sidecar prevents re-summing already-merged content on TruncateNode failure/retry (F1); the floor prevents segment-number reuse across a successful truncation (F2).

first_ever_compaction_unaffected.evidence: readSegmentFloor returns os.IsNotExist for a node dir with no floor file (never truncated); latestSegmentNum's existing fallback to (0,false,nil) is unchanged for this case. Exercised by TestCompaction's existing subtests and TestTruncateNode's no-op-on-first-call assertion, both green.

floor_persists_for_node_lifetime.evidence: getOrOpenWriter always calls wal.OpenWriter fresh (never bypasses latestSegmentNum) whenever a writer isn't cached -- which is guaranteed after TruncateNode closes and evicts the cached writer. Every subsequent AppendEdge for that fileID therefore consults the floor via OpenWriter->latestSegmentNum. TestCompaction_SecondAppendAfterSuccessfulCompactionIsNotLost's 3-round structure (append/compact/append/compact/append/compact) directly exercises 'does this keep working, not just once' and passes.

concurrent_access.evidence: TruncateNode holds l.mu.Lock() for its full duration (floor-write + removal), which blocks any concurrent getOrOpenWriter call from creating/reopening a writer for the same fileID during that window -- WriteSegmentFloor introduces no NEW race here. However, EdgeLog.ReadNodeAfter (called by Compact to decide what's 'incoming') does NOT take l.mu at all, and an already-cached wal.Writer's own Append() also does not consult EdgeLog.mu -- so a concurrent AppendEdge racing Compact's read-then-truncate sequence could still, in principle, have a freshly-appended segment swept up and deleted by TruncateNode's own independent re-listing before ever being merged into graph.dat. This is a genuine, pre-existing gap, but traced and confirmed IDENTICAL in severity and mechanism in commit 9850083 (pre-F2-fix) and even ebbc1ff (original) -- TruncateNode has always unconditionally deleted whatever it lists at truncate time, regardless of whether Compact's earlier read saw the same set. Not introduced or worsened by ed57468's floor mechanism. Matches the already-documented, explicitly non-blocking carry-forward item from 010-verification ('Compact/TruncateNode lock ordering, ReadNode not holding l.mu across AppendEdge'). Non-blocking, but flagged again since it remains open across three fix cycles now.

test_coverage.evidence: Two new regression tests directly target F2's exact scenario and the F1/F2 interaction seam. Both independently re-verified via a redone git-worktree revert-experiment (not merely trusted from the commit message): fail against pre-fix 9850083 with the exact silent-drop signature (Weight stuck at first value, second edge never merged), pass against ed57468.

confidence.evidence: High confidence the specific F2 defect is fixed and the F1/F2 interaction is sound, verified via direct fault-injection experiments beyond what the implementer's own tests cover (crash-window, torn-floor-write, monotonicity, sidecar/floor cross-consistency). Residual, non-blocking, pre-existing lock-ordering gap noted above (present across all 3 commits at this seam, not new).

performance.evidence: WriteSegmentFloor is a single small O(1) atomic file write per truncated node, negligible relative to Compact's existing O(all nodes + all log entries) cost. Node directories are no longer deleted (retained indefinitely as floor-marker holders), which is a small, permanent, unbounded-count directory-entry accumulation over a long-lived engine's lifetime -- worth tracking but not a blocking regression for this subtask's scope.

maintainability.evidence: Doc comments in all three touched files are thorough and explicitly cross-reference the F1/F2 history, including the specific alternative design considered and rejected (sidecar-clear-on-success) and why. This is the clearest-documented of the three fix cycles at this seam.

items: Address the carry-forward EdgeLog lock-ordering gap (ReadNodeAfter not holding l.mu, TruncateNode independently re-listing segments rather than truncating exactly the set Compact observed) -- now flagged across three consecutive verification passes at this same seam. Recommend either having Compact pass the exact segment set/maxSeg it read into TruncateNode (truncate exactly what was merged, not whatever exists now), or having ReadNodeAfter and TruncateNode share a lock scope for a given node across a single Compact iteration.

Node directories now persist forever (as floor-marker holders) even after all their edges are permanently compacted away and never appended to again -- consider a periodic sweep or documenting this as accepted, unbounded (but very small, one-int-file-per-historically-truncated-node) growth.

The theoretical case of a genuinely PARTIAL segment-removal failure inside TruncateNode's removal loop (some segments removed, some not, e.g. via ENOSPC/EIO rather than a uniform directory-permission failure) combined with a live AppendEdge landing on the still-present, already-floor-recorded segment number before the next successful retry was traced analytically (not reproducible via this repo's chmod-based fault injection, since directory-level permission failures block all removes in the loop uniformly) and could in principle still silently lose the newly-appended data. This exists identically in all three commits at this seam (not introduced by ed57468) and is a narrower case of the same lock-ordering gap above; recommend covering explicitly if a fault-injection harness is ever added.

adversarial_checks_summary: 1. Revert-experiment independently redone via git worktree at 9850083 (not trusting the commit message's claimed transcript): extracted both new regression tests in isolation into the pre-fix worktree, confirmed both FAIL there with F2's exact silent-drop signature (Weight stuck at the first appended value; second/third edges never merged), and PASS against ed57468 on the main working tree. Genuine, reproducible fix.

2. Crash window (floor-write success, removal not yet complete) traced via a direct fault-injection unit test (TestFloorCrash_LeftoverSegmentWinsOverFloor): confirmed OpenWriter resumes from the still-present segment's own real number, never the floor, so leftover un-removed data is never silently skipped or double-counted -- matches the documented degrade-to-F1's-safe-retry-window claim exactly.

3. WriteSegmentFloor's own atomicity (temp+fsync+rename) verified via direct fault injection (TestFloorCrash_TornTempFileIgnored): a leftover, never-renamed temp file is correctly ignored, never observed as a torn/partial floor value. Monotonicity verified directly (TestFloorMonotonic): a lower floor computed from a stale listing never regresses an already-published higher floor.

4. Floor persistence across a node's full lifetime confirmed: getOrOpenWriter always re-consults wal.OpenWriter/latestSegmentNum after TruncateNode evicts the cached writer, so every future AppendEdge for that fileID sees the floor. Exercised directly by the 3-round structure of TestCompaction_SecondAppendAfterSuccessfulCompactionIsNotLost.

5. Sidecar/floor consistency verified via a new constructed multi-round test (TestSidecarFloorConsistency_MultiSegmentTruncate): confirmed floor == sidecar-recorded-segment+1 exactly across 5 append/compact rounds; the two mechanisms coexist correctly and neither is redundant (sidecar guards F1's retry-idempotency, floor guards F2's segment-reuse).

6. First-ever-compaction (no floor file exists) path confirmed unaffected: readSegmentFloor's os.IsNotExist path falls through to the original (0,false,nil) behavior unchanged; TestTruncateNode's no-op assertion and TestCompaction's base subtests (unmodified, still passing) both exercise this.

7. Concurrent access analyzed: WriteSegmentFloor itself introduces no new race (TruncateNode holds l.mu.Lock() for its entire duration, blocking concurrent writer creation). However, a genuine, non-blocking, PRE-EXISTING gap was reconfirmed: ReadNodeAfter does not hold l.mu, and TruncateNode independently re-lists segments rather than truncating exactly what Compact read -- traced and confirmed present with identical mechanism/severity in 9850083 and ebbc1ff, i.e. NOT introduced or worsened by this fix cycle. This matches 010-verification's own carry-forward, non-blocking item.

8. Commands run: go build ./... (clean), go vet ./... (clean), gofmt -l . (clean, no output), go test ./graph/... -run TestCompaction -race -count=10 -timeout 15m (PASS), go test ./graph/... -run TestTruncateNode -race -count=10 -timeout 15m (PASS), go test ./wal/... -race -count=5 -timeout 10m (PASS), go test ./graph/... -count=1 -timeout 10m (PASS), go test ./... -count=1 -timeout 25m (all packages green except the pre-known, explicitly-anticipated TestReaderDuringSplit flake in engine/split, unrelated to this subtask).

next_steps.reason: F2 is genuinely fixed and independently re-verified (not merely trusted from the implementer's transcript). No new seam bug found at the F1/F2 interaction despite maximum adversarial scrutiny including direct fault-injection experiments beyond the implementer's own test suite. One non-blocking, PRE-EXISTING (not new) lock-ordering gap remains open across all three commits at this seam and is called out again as a possible_improvement/carry-forward item for a future subtask, not as a blocker for 3.1.3 or 3.1.4.
